docs.title: Modernization Atlas: Project uiao-core
docs.text: Welcome to the authoritative guide for the agency's infrastructure transformation. The uiao-core initiative replaces legacy, fragmented silos with a unified, programmable architecture.

docs.title: End-to-End Demo
docs.text: Run uiao generate-all to transform YAML canon into OSCAL JSON, Markdown docs, DOCX, PPTX, and a CycloneDX SBOM in a single command. See the Pipeline documentation for a full stage-by-stage breakdown.

docs.title: Mission-to-Tech Alignment
docs.text: Our modernization strategy is built to deliver three primary outcomes: Agility, Security, and Compliance.

docs.title: Core Strategic Pillars
docs.text:
• Identity Plane: Moving to a Zero-Trust identity model via Azure AD and Cisco ISE.
  • Addressing Plane: Automating the lifecycle of every IP and DNS record via Infoblox.
  • Overlay Plane: Implementing a programmable fabric that abstracts the physical network.
  • Telemetry Plane: Creating a closed-loop feedback system using Splunk and Azure Monitor.

Executive Summary: This Atlas is a "Living Document." It is generated directly from our codebase, ensuring that the documentation is always synchronized with the authoritative technical standards.

docs.title: SSP Inventory
docs.text:
The SSP system-inventory section is auto-generated from data/inventory-items.yml. Each inventory item links back to a component in the Component Definition via its component-id prop.

Inventory ID Description Component Link Vendor inv-entra-id Microsoft Entra ID - Identity Provider component-identity Microsoft inv-sentinel Microsoft Sentinel - SIEM Platform component-telemetry Microsoft inv-cisco-sdwan Cisco SD-WAN - Network Segmentation component-network Cisco inv-infoblox-ddi InfoBlox DDI - DNS/DHCP/IPAM component-addressing InfoBlox ---

docs.title: Continuous Monitoring
docs.text: Real-time telemetry from Microsoft Sentinel (SIEM) is integrated with POA&M status tracking. When a monitoring signal fires, the corresponding POA&M finding is automatically enriched with the signal source, type, and timestamp so that compliance documentation stays synchronized with operational security posture.

docs.title: Active Monitoring Sources
docs.text: Source Type Controls Covered Microsoft Sentinel SIEM AC-2, AC-6, IA-2, SC-7

docs.title: Telemetry Signal → POA&M Mapping
docs.text:
Signal Control Trigger Description identity_anomaly AC-2 open Anomalous identity behavior detected mfa_bypass_attempt IA-2 open MFA bypass or fallback detected network_segmentation_alert SC-7 open Unexpected cross-segment traffic detected privileged_access_escalation AC-6 open Unauthorized privilege escalation detected

When a signal's poam_status_trigger is open, the relevant POA&M item receives:

  • monitoring-source prop — name of the originating SIEM (e.g., Microsoft Sentinel)
  • signal-type prop — machine-readable signal identifier (e.g., identity_anomaly)
  • last-signal-date prop — ISO 8601 timestamp of the most recent enrichment run
  • continuous-monitoring remark — human-readable note linking the telemetry to the finding

Enrichment is performed automatically by scripts/generate_poam.py (via monitoring_enabled=True) and can also be run standalone with scripts/update_poam_from_monitoring.py. Use the --dry-run flag to preview changes without modifying any artifacts.

docs.title: Control Plane Architecture
docs.text: Field Value Version 1.0 Date 2026-03 Classification CUI/FOUO Source Plane(s) Identity, Network, Addressing, Telemetry, Security, Management Document Type Architectural Specification (00_Core)

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document defines the six control planes that form the foundation of the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay (UIAO) architecture. It establishes the authoritative structure, responsibilities, boundaries, and interactions of each plane, ensuring deterministic behavior across identity, network, addressing, telemetry, security, and management domains.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Canonical definitions of all six control planes
  • Responsibilities and boundaries for each plane
  • Cross-plane interactions
  • Runtime alignment with UIAO's conversation-centric model
  • Compliance and governance anchors

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Implementation-specific configurations
  • Vendor-specific deployment guides
  • Project plans (covered in modernization appendices)
  • Diagrams (referenced but not embedded)

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
This document defines all six UIAO control planes and establishes their relationships.

Plane Role Identity Primary source of truth for identity, assurance, and access Network Routing, segmentation, and path selection Addressing Deterministic IPAM, DNS, DHCP, and identity-derived addressing Telemetry & Location Real-time signals for routing, security, and compliance Security & Compliance Zero Trust enforcement, FedRAMP alignment, TIC 3.0 Management Governance, drift detection, CMDB, device compliance

All other UIAO documents reference this plane model.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text:
The six control planes operate according to the Seven Core Concepts of UIAO:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit
  2. Identity as the root namespace
  3. Deterministic addressing
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay
  5. Telemetry as control
  6. Embedded governance & automation
  7. Public service first

These concepts are frozen and must appear identically across all UIAO documents.

docs.title: 6.1 Overview
docs.text: The UIAO architecture replaces legacy perimeter-centric models with a federated, identity-driven, telemetry-informed control plane system. Each plane is authoritative for a specific domain but interoperates through deterministic, identity-anchored relationships.

docs.title: Identity Control Plane
docs.text:
• Authoritative System: Microsoft Entra ID
  • Responsibilities:
  • Identity lifecycle (joiner/mover/leaver)
  • Authentication and assurance
  • Conditional Access
  • Privileged Identity Management
  • Device trust (via Intune)
  • Identity-derived addressing and policy
  • Inputs: HRIS, device compliance, certificate status
  • Outputs: Tokens, claims, identity attributes, assurance signals

docs.title: Network Control Plane
docs.text:
• Authoritative System: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN
  • Responsibilities:
  • Routing and path selection
  • Identity-aware segmentation
  • Cloud OnRamp for M365
  • DIA and branch modernization
  • mTLS-anchored overlay enforcement
  • Inputs: Identity attributes, addressing, telemetry
  • Outputs: Segmentation decisions, path selection, overlay tunnels

docs.title: Addressing Control Plane
docs.text:
• Authoritative System: Infoblox BloxOne IPAM
  • Responsibilities:
  • Deterministic IP assignment
  • DNS/DHCP modernization
  • Cloud/on-prem reconciliation
  • Identity-derived addressing
  • Location inference
  • Inputs: Identity attributes, network boundaries
  • Outputs: IP allocations, DNS records, DHCP leases

docs.title: Telemetry & Location Control Plane
docs.text:
• Authoritative Systems: M365 Network Telemetry, SD-WAN telemetry, Defender/Intune, DNS telemetry
  • Responsibilities:
  • Real-time conversation-level telemetry
  • Location inference (INR, IPAM, SD-WAN)
  • E911 readiness
  • SIEM ingestion
  • Automated control inputs
  • Inputs: Network flows, identity events, device posture
  • Outputs: Assurance signals, routing modifiers, compliance indicators

docs.title: Security & Compliance Plane
docs.text:
• Authoritative Systems: TIC 3.0, Zero Trust, FedRAMP 20x
  • Responsibilities:
  • Zero Trust enforcement
  • FedRAMP 20x telemetry-based validation
  • NIST 800-63 identity governance
  • Policy enforcement across planes
  • Inputs: Identity, telemetry, addressing, device posture
  • Outputs: Allow/deny decisions, audit artifacts, compliance posture

docs.title: Management Plane
docs.text:
• Authoritative Systems: ServiceNow, Microsoft Intune
  • Responsibilities:
  • CMDB and asset inventory
  • Drift detection and remediation
  • Device compliance enforcement
  • Change control and incident lifecycle
  • Continuous Authorization (CA-7)
  • Inputs: Telemetry, configuration baselines, identity lifecycle
  • Outputs: Governance actions, remediation workflows, audit trails

docs.title: 7. Runtime Model
docs.text: UIAO operates on conversations as the atomic unit.

docs.title: 7.1 Conversation Flow
docs.text:
1. Identity initiates
  2. Addressing binds
  3. Certificates authenticate
  4. Overlay establishes path
  5. Telemetry validates
  6. Policy evaluates continuously

docs.title: 7.2 Determinism
docs.text: Given identical identity, boundary, telemetry, and assurance inputs, the system must produce the same decision across clouds, agencies, and implementations.

docs.title: 7.3 Continuous Evaluation
docs.text: Telemetry modifies routing, access, and segmentation decisions in real time.

docs.title: 8.1 FedRAMP 20x Alignment
docs.text:
• Class C (Moderate)
  • Telemetry-based validation
  • OSCAL machine-readability

docs.text:
Mapped via the canonical crosswalk:

Control ID Control Name AC-4 Information Flow Enforcement (segmentation) IA-2 Identification and Authentication IA-5 Authenticator Management CM-8 Information System Component Inventory SC-8 Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity (mTLS) CA-7 Continuous Monitoring SI-4 Information System Monitoring (telemetry)

docs.title: Unified Architecture Specification
docs.text: Field Value Version 1.0 Date 2026-03 Classification CUI/FOUO Source Plane(s) Identity, Network, Addressing, Telemetry, Security, Management Document Type Architectural Specification (00_Core)

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document defines the unified architecture of the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay (UIAO) system. It integrates all six control planes into a single, coherent, identity-driven, telemetry-informed modernization framework. It serves as the architectural foundation for project plans, compliance mappings, modernization timelines, and executive briefings.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Unified architectural model across all six control planes
  • Cross-plane interactions and dependencies
  • Core concepts and runtime behavior
  • Architectural principles and modernization rationale
  • Frozen state analysis and required state transitions

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Detailed plane-specific specifications (see 00_ControlPlaneArchitecture.md)
  • Implementation guides
  • Vendor-specific deployment instructions
  • Project plan details (covered in modernization appendices)

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
This document describes the unified behavior of all six UIAO control planes:

Plane Role in Unified Architecture Identity Root namespace and assurance engine Network Routing, segmentation, and overlay transport Addressing Deterministic IPAM and DNS/DHCP authority Telemetry & Location Real-time signals for routing, security, and compliance Security & Compliance Zero Trust enforcement and FedRAMP alignment Management Governance, drift detection, CMDB, device compliance

The unified architecture is the composition of these planes operating deterministically.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text:
The unified architecture is governed by the Seven Core Concepts, which must appear identically across all UIAO documents:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit
  2. Identity as the root namespace
  3. Deterministic addressing
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay
  5. Telemetry as control
  6. Embedded governance & automation
  7. Public service first

These concepts define the architectural philosophy and runtime behavior of UIAO.

docs.title: 6.1 Overview
docs.text:
The UIAO architecture replaces legacy perimeter-centric, device-centric, and location-centric models with a federated, identity-driven, telemetry-informed control plane system. Each plane is authoritative for its domain, but all planes operate as a single, deterministic system.

The architecture is designed to:

  • Eliminate TIC 2.0 bottlenecks
  • Replace static addressing with identity-derived addressing
  • Replace siloed logs with conversation-level telemetry
  • Replace manual governance with automated enforcement
  • Replace perimeter trust with continuous Zero Trust evaluation

docs.title: 6.2 Architectural Principles
docs.text:
The unified architecture is governed by the following principles:

  • Zero Trust by default
  • Identity as the new perimeter
  • Telemetry as the truth source
  • Cloud-first routing
  • Incremental modernization (no big-bang)
  • FedRAMP-aligned controls
  • Modular, extensible architecture

These principles are mandatory across all UIAO implementations.

docs.title: Identity <-> Network
docs.text: Identity provides segmentation attributes; network enforces identity-aware routing.

docs.title: Network <-> Addressing
docs.text: Addressing provides deterministic IPs; network uses them for segmentation and telemetry correlation.

docs.title: Addressing <-> Telemetry
docs.text: IPAM provides identity-derived IPs; telemetry uses them for location inference and conversation correlation.

docs.title: Telemetry <-> Security
docs.text: Telemetry provides real-time assurance; security enforces Zero Trust decisions.

docs.title: Security <-> Management
docs.text: Security defines policy; management enforces drift detection and remediation.

docs.title: Management <-> Identity
docs.text:
Device compliance and lifecycle events feed identity assurance.

This creates a closed-loop, self-governing architecture.

docs.title: 6.4 Frozen State Analysis
docs.text:
The unified architecture addresses the following legacy constraints:

Domain Frozen State Required State Identity On-prem AD, siloed Unified identity graph Addressing Static spreadsheets Deterministic identity-derived addressing Network Security L3/L4 firewalls Identity-aware segmentation Endpoint Mixed tooling Unified posture signal App Delivery Monolithic, local auth Workload identity Telemetry Siloed logs Conversation-level correlation Governance Email/ticket-based Automated enforcement Data Protection Manual classification Data-aware routing

The unified architecture provides the required state.

docs.title: 7. Runtime Model
docs.text: UIAO operates on conversations as the atomic unit of runtime behavior.

docs.title: 7.1 Conversation Flow
docs.text:
1. Identity initiates
  2. Addressing binds
  3. Certificates authenticate
  4. Overlay establishes path
  5. Telemetry validates
  6. Policy evaluates continuously

docs.title: 7.2 Deterministic Behavior
docs.text:
Given identical identity, boundary, telemetry, and assurance inputs, the system must produce the same decision across:

  • Clouds
  • Agencies
  • Implementations

docs.title: 7.3 Continuous Evaluation
docs.text: Telemetry modifies routing, access, and segmentation decisions in real time.

docs.title: 7.4 Assurance Signals
docs.text:
• Identity assurance
  • Device posture
  • Path quality
  • Location inference
  • Certificate validity

These signals drive continuous policy evaluation.

docs.title: 8.1 FedRAMP 20x Alignment
docs.text:
The unified architecture supports:

  • Class C (Moderate)
  • Telemetry-based validation
  • OSCAL machine-readability
  • Automated evidence generation

docs.title: Canon Specification
docs.text: Field Value Version 1.0 Date 2026-03 Classification CUI/FOUO Source Plane(s) Identity, Network, Addressing, Telemetry, Security, Management Document Type Canon Volume (01_Canon)

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document defines the UIAO Canon — the doctrinal, architectural, and operational framework that explains why federal IT is structurally frozen and what must change to achieve a modern, identity-driven, telemetry-informed enterprise. It establishes the modernization canon, the source-of-authority domains, the runtime model, and the conditions required for deterministic, cross-agency interoperability.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Canon thesis and modernization rationale
  • Structural constraints of federal IT
  • The 17-Point Modernization Canon
  • Source-of-Authority domains
  • Runtime model and determinism
  • Identity fragmentation analysis
  • Compliance drivers and cost of inaction

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Plane-specific architecture (see 00_ControlPlaneArchitecture.md)
  • Unified architecture model (see 01_UnifiedArchitecture.md)
  • Project plans and timelines
  • Vendor-specific implementation details

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
The canon governs all six UIAO control planes:

Plane Canonical Role Identity Root namespace and assurance engine Network Transport, segmentation, and overlay Addressing Deterministic IPAM and DNS/DHCP authority Telemetry & Location Truth source for runtime decisions Security & Compliance Zero Trust enforcement and FedRAMP alignment Management Governance, drift detection, and continuous authorization

The canon defines the principles that govern these planes.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text:
The canon is anchored in the Seven Core Concepts, which define the philosophical and operational foundation of UIAO:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit
  2. Identity as the root namespace
  3. Deterministic addressing
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay
  5. Telemetry as control
  6. Embedded governance & automation
  7. Public service first

These concepts are frozen and must appear identically across all UIAO documents.

docs.title: 6.1 Core Thesis
docs.text:
Federal IT is structurally frozen at the Client/Server L2-L4 perimeter era.

Identity-forward modernization — where identity becomes the root namespace and primary security perimeter — is the only viable path forward.

docs.title: 6.2 Why Incremental Patching Fails
docs.text:
Federal environments are constrained by five systemic failures:

Problem Frozen State Required State inverted-identity Authenticate once Continuous identity signal backwards-trust Inside = trusted Trust nothing, verify everything disconnected-telemetry Siloed logs Conversation-level correlation manual-governance Human review cycles Automated enforcement at machine speed no-data-control-plane Perimeter-based data protection Data-level controls that travel with data

Incremental patching cannot overcome these structural constraints.

docs.title: 6.3 The 17-Point Modernization Canon
docs.text: The canon is organized into three tiers:

docs.title: Tier 1 — Historical Foundations
docs.text:
1. Evolution of compute, networking, and cybersecurity
  2. Federal freeze points
  3. Legacy perimeter architectures
  4. Fragmented identity regimes

docs.title: Tier 2 — Structural Constraints
docs.text:
1. Bureaucratic overlays
  2. Funding model mismatches
  3. L2-L4 vs L5-L7 architectural gaps
  4. Siloed governance and telemetry
  5. Source-of-truth fragmentation

docs.title: Tier 3 — Modern Requirements
docs.text:
1. Telemetry and location as mandatory inputs
  2. Identity as the root namespace
  3. Deterministic addressing
  4. Certificate-anchored overlays
  5. AI-assisted correlation
  6. Inter-agency truth fabric
  7. Data as perimeter
  8. Modernized risk models

These 17 points form the doctrinal spine of UIAO.

docs.title: UIAO FedRAMP 20x Compliance Crosswalk
docs.text: Field Value Version 1.0 Date 2026-03 Classification CUI/FOUO Source Plane(s) Identity, Network, Addressing, Telemetry, Security, Management Document Type Compliance Crosswalk (02_Appendices)

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document provides the authoritative FedRAMP 20x compliance crosswalk for the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay (UIAO) architecture. It maps UIAO's core concepts, control planes, and runtime model to the NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 controls required for a FedRAMP Moderate (Class C) authorization under the 20x telemetry-based validation framework.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Mapping of UIAO concepts to NIST 800-53 Rev 5 controls
  • KSI (Key Security Indicator) alignment
  • Evidence sources for telemetry-based validation
  • Mandatory 2026 infrastructure requirements
  • Audit anchors and continuous monitoring expectations

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Implementation-specific configurations
  • Vendor deployment guides
  • Project plan sequencing (covered in modernization timeline)

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
This crosswalk spans all six UIAO control planes:

Plane Compliance Role Identity Identity assurance, MFA, lifecycle governance Network Segmentation, routing, overlay security Addressing Deterministic IPAM, DNS/DHCP integrity Telemetry & Location Continuous monitoring, KSI generation Security & Compliance Zero Trust enforcement, FedRAMP alignment Management CMDB, drift detection, remediation workflows

Each plane contributes evidence to FedRAMP 20x telemetry validation.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text:
The crosswalk maps the Seven Core Concepts to NIST controls:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit → AC-4, SI-4
  2. Identity as the root namespace → IA-2, AC-2
  3. Deterministic addressing → CM-8, AC-4
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay → SC-8, IA-5
  5. Telemetry as control → CA-7, SI-4
  6. Embedded governance & automation → CM-2, CM-3
  7. Public service first → PT-2 (privacy and minimization)

These mappings are frozen and must appear identically across all UIAO compliance documents.

docs.title: 6.1 FedRAMP 20x Overview
docs.text:
FedRAMP 20x replaces narrative-based compliance with telemetry-based validation, requiring:

  • Machine-readable OSCAL packages
  • Real-time Key Security Indicators (KSIs)
  • Automated evidence generation
  • Continuous monitoring (CA-7)
  • Identity-anchored access control
  • Deterministic asset inventory

UIAO is designed explicitly to satisfy these requirements.

docs.title: 6.2 Fundamental Concept Mapping
docs.text:
UIAO Concept NIST Control KSI Category Evidence Source Conversation as Atomic Unit AC-4 KSI-CNA SD-WAN flow telemetry Identity as Root Namespace IA-2 / AC-2 KSI-IAM Entra ID & CyberArk logs Deterministic Addressing CM-8 / AC-4 KSI-PIY Infoblox BloxOne DDI API Certificate-Anchored Overlay SC-8 / IA-5 KSI-SVC SD-WAN mTLS configuration Telemetry as Control CA-7 / SI-4 KSI-MLA M365 & SD-WAN telemetry Embedded Governance CM-2 KSI-CMT GitHub YAML baseline Public Service First PT-2 KSI-CED Identity & overlay minimization

These mappings are canonical and must not be altered.

docs.title: 7. Runtime Model
docs.text: UIAO's runtime model directly supports FedRAMP 20x telemetry validation.

docs.title: 7.1 Conversation-Level Telemetry
docs.text:
Every conversation produces:

  • Identity metadata
  • Addressing metadata
  • Certificate metadata
  • Path and QoS telemetry
  • Security and assurance signals

docs.title: 7.2 Deterministic Evidence
docs.text: Given identical inputs, UIAO produces identical telemetry outputs — enabling reproducible compliance.

docs.title: 7.3 Continuous Monitoring
docs.text:
Telemetry feeds:

  • CA-7 continuous monitoring
  • SI-4 anomaly detection
  • AC-4 segmentation enforcement
  • SC-8 certificate validation

This satisfies FedRAMP's requirement for machine-generated evidence.

docs.title: 8.1 Mandatory 2026 Infrastructure Requirements
docs.text:
ID Requirement Status Deadline NTC-0003 Automated Security Inbox Required 2026-01-05 RFC-0024 OSCAL Machine-Readability Required 2026-09-30 M-24-15 Phishing-Resistant MFA Required 2026-09-30

UIAO satisfies all three through Entra ID, SD-WAN telemetry, and GitHub-based governance.

docs.title: 8.2 Audit Anchor Summary
docs.text:
UIAO provides continuous telemetry via:

  • Identity Pillar: Entra ID MFA, PIV/FIDO2
  • Addressing Pillar: Infoblox deterministic IPAM
  • Overlay Pillar: SD-WAN mTLS service chain
  • Telemetry Pillar: M365, SD-WAN, DNS, endpoint signals

These anchors form the evidence base for FedRAMP 20x.

docs.title: 8.3 KSI Definitions
docs.text:
KSI Description KSI-IAM Identity authentication logs (Entra ID) KSI-PIY Deterministic asset inventory (Infoblox) KSI-MLA Network health & path telemetry KSI-SVC Certificate enforcement (mTLS) KSI-CMT Baseline drift detection (GitHub YAML) KSI-CNA Packet-level identity metadata KSI-CED Data minimization enforcement

These KSIs are mandatory for FedRAMP 20x validation.

docs.text:

  • SD-WAN HLD/LLD (Workstream B)
  • IPAM modernization (Workstream C)
  • Telemetry integration (Workstream D)

docs.title: Downstream Dependencies

  • FedRAMP annual assessment
  • Continuous monitoring dashboards
  • Governance workflows

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document provides the authoritative summary of how the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay (UIAO) architecture satisfies the requirements of FedRAMP 20x Phase 2. It explains the modernization drivers, architectural alignment, control-plane contributions, and compliance outcomes required for a telemetry-based, identity-anchored, Zero Trust-aligned federal enterprise.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Phase 2 modernization drivers
  • Architectural alignment across all six control planes
  • Core concepts supporting FedRAMP 20x
  • Frozen state compliance risks
  • Compliance outcomes and mission impact

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Detailed crosswalk mappings (see 03_FedRAMP20x_Crosswalk.md)
  • Project plan sequencing (see 08_ModernizationTimeline.md)
  • Vendor-specific deployment instructions

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
FedRAMP 20x Phase 2 compliance is achieved through coordinated operation of all six UIAO control planes:

Plane Phase 2 Compliance Role Identity MFA, lifecycle governance, identity assurance Network Cloud-first routing, segmentation, mTLS overlay Addressing Deterministic IPAM, DNS/DHCP modernization Telemetry and Location Real-time KSI generation, INR/E911 readiness Security and Compliance Zero Trust enforcement, FedRAMP alignment Management Drift detection, CMDB integrity, device compliance

Each plane contributes mandatory telemetry and evidence for FedRAMP 20x validation.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text:
FedRAMP 20x Phase 2 is supported by the Seven Core Concepts of UIAO:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit
  2. Identity as the root namespace
  3. Deterministic addressing
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay
  5. Telemetry as control
  6. Embedded governance and automation
  7. Public service first

These concepts ensure that compliance is continuous, automated, and identity-anchored.

docs.title: 6.1 Modernization Drivers
docs.text:
The agency's legacy environment exhibits structural constraints that prevent compliance with FedRAMP 20x Phase 2:

  • TIC 2.0 hairpinning degrades M365 performance
  • Identity anchored in on-prem AD with inconsistent governance
  • Fragmented IPAM across spreadsheets and disconnected tools
  • Siloed telemetry preventing conversation-level correlation
  • Manual governance processes incompatible with continuous monitoring
  • Perimeter-centric security models unable to enforce Zero Trust

These constraints create direct mission impact:

  • Poor cloud performance
  • Increased cyber risk
  • Compliance gaps (TIC 3.0, FedRAMP 20x, SCuBA)
  • Operational inefficiencies

UIAO resolves these constraints through identity-driven, telemetry-informed modernization.

docs.title: Identity Control Plane
docs.text:
• Entra ID as authoritative identity
  • ICAM governance (NIST 800-63, OMB M-19-17)
  • Conditional Access enforcing device trust
  • PIM for privileged access
  • Automated lifecycle (joiner/mover/leaver)

docs.title: Network Control Plane
docs.text:
• Cisco SD-WAN for cloud-first routing
  • Identity-aware segmentation
  • Cloud OnRamp for M365
  • INR integration for location-aware routing

docs.title: Addressing Control Plane
docs.text:
• Infoblox IPAM replacing spreadsheets
  • Deterministic, identity-derived addressing
  • Unified DNS/DHCP across cloud and on-prem
  • Accurate telemetry correlation

docs.title: Telemetry and Location Control Plane
docs.text:
• M365, SD-WAN, DNS, endpoint telemetry
  • Conversation-level correlation
  • E911 dynamic location mapping
  • IPAM-based location inference

docs.title: Security and Compliance Plane
docs.text:
• TIC 3.0 Cloud + Branch
  • Zero Trust enforcement
  • FedRAMP 20x telemetry validation
  • NIST 800-63 identity governance

Together, these planes produce continuous, machine-generated evidence.

docs.title: 7. Runtime Model
docs.text: UIAO's runtime model directly satisfies FedRAMP 20x Phase 2 requirements.

docs.title: 7.1 Conversation-Level Telemetry
docs.text:
Every interaction produces:

  • Identity metadata
  • Addressing metadata
  • Certificate metadata
  • Path and QoS telemetry
  • Device posture
  • Location inference

docs.title: 7.2 Deterministic Behavior
docs.text: Given identical inputs, UIAO produces identical telemetry outputs — enabling reproducible compliance.

docs.title: 7.3 Continuous Evaluation
docs.text:
Telemetry drives:

  • Routing decisions
  • Access decisions
  • Segmentation decisions
  • Compliance posture

This satisfies CA-7 continuous monitoring and SI-4 anomaly detection.

docs.title: 8.1 Frozen State Compliance Risks
docs.text:
Domain Frozen State Compliance Risk Identity Siloed AD Inconsistent governance Addressing Static spreadsheets No correlation or inventory integrity Network Security L3/L4 firewalls No identity-aware segmentation Endpoint Mixed tooling No unified posture signal App Delivery Local auth No workload identity Telemetry Siloed logs No conversation-level visibility Governance Email/tickets No automated enforcement Data Protection Manual classification No data-aware routing

UIAO resolves these risks through deterministic, identity-anchored modernization.

docs.title: 8.2 Compliance Outcomes
docs.text:
UIAO delivers measurable improvements:

  • Performance: Cloud-first routing improves M365 performance
  • Security: Identity-driven segmentation reduces attack surface
  • Compliance: Telemetry enables FedRAMP 20x validation
  • Governance: Automated workflows replace manual tickets
  • Mission Readiness: Faster, more reliable, more secure services

These outcomes satisfy the intent and requirements of FedRAMP 20x Phase 2.

docs.title: Upstream Dependencies
docs.text:
• Identity modernization (Workstream A)
  • SD-WAN HLD/LLD (Workstream B)
  • IPAM modernization (Workstream C)
  • Telemetry integration (Workstream D)

docs.title: Downstream Dependencies
docs.text:
• TIC 3.0 Cloud Use Case Package
  • TIC 3.0 Branch Use Case Package
  • CDM/CLAW integration
  • Annual FedRAMP assessment

docs.title: UIAO Management Stack — FedRAMP Control Definitions
docs.text: Field Value Version 1.0 Date 2026-03 Classification CUI/FOUO Source Plane(s) Management, Identity, Security, Telemetry Document Type Architectural Specification (00_Core)

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document defines the Management Plane of the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay (UIAO) architecture. It establishes ServiceNow and Microsoft Intune as the authoritative governance systems responsible for drift detection, configuration integrity, device compliance, continuous authorization, and operational lifecycle management. It also maps their capabilities to NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 controls required for FedRAMP 20x (Class C) authorization.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Canonical definition of the Management Plane
  • ServiceNow and Intune roles, responsibilities, and evidence sources
  • NIST 800-53 Rev 5 control mappings
  • Integration bindings across UIAO control planes
  • Governance and drift-remediation workflows

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Vendor-specific deployment instructions
  • Detailed CMDB schema design
  • Device configuration baselines (covered in Intune profiles)

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
The Management Plane governs and enforces configuration integrity across all six UIAO control planes:

Plane Management Role Identity Device trust, lifecycle synchronization Network Overlay drift detection, segmentation enforcement Addressing IPAM reconciliation, authoritative inventory Telemetry Continuous monitoring, evidence generation Security & Compliance Zero Trust enforcement, FedRAMP alignment Management CMDB, change control, compliance posture

ServiceNow and Intune jointly form the authoritative Management Plane.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text:
The Management Plane operationalizes the Seven Core Concepts:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit → Incident, change, and configuration events tied to identity and device
  2. Identity as the root namespace → Device trust and configuration bound to identity
  3. Deterministic addressing → CMDB reconciliation with IPAM as source of truth
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay → Certificate drift detection and renewal workflows
  5. Telemetry as control → Compliance signals drive access decisions
  6. Embedded governance & automation → Automated remediation replaces manual tickets
  7. Public service first → Reduced downtime, faster remediation, higher reliability

docs.title: 6.1 Overview
docs.text:
The Management Plane ensures that UIAO remains drift-resistant, continuously authorized, and operationally compliant. It integrates:

  • ServiceNow → System of record for incidents, changes, CMDB, and governance
  • Microsoft Intune → Device compliance, configuration baselines, and hardware/software inventory

Together, they provide:

  • Authoritative asset inventory (CM-8)
  • Baseline configuration enforcement (CM-2, CM-6)
  • Drift detection and remediation (CM-3, CA-7)
  • Device trust for Conditional Access (AC-19)
  • Evidence for FedRAMP 20x telemetry validation

docs.title: Role
docs.text:
ServiceNow is the system of record for:

  • Incident lifecycle management
  • Change control
  • CMDB asset inventory
  • Continuous authorization
  • Overlay drift detection
  • Automated remediation workflows

docs.title: 7. Runtime Model
docs.text: The Management Plane enforces continuous authorization across all UIAO conversations.

docs.title: 7.1 Continuous Evaluation
docs.text:
• Device posture
  • Certificate validity
  • Configuration baseline
  • Identity assurance
  • Overlay integrity

docs.title: 7.2 Drift Detection
docs.text:
Drift is detected when:

  • A device falls out of compliance
  • A configuration baseline changes
  • An overlay tunnel deviates from the approved baseline
  • IPAM records diverge from CMDB
  • Certificates expire or mismatch

docs.title: 7.3 Automated Remediation
docs.text:
Triggered via:

  • ServiceNow change workflows
  • Intune compliance enforcement
  • Conditional Access policies
  • Certificate renewal pipelines
  • IPAM reconciliation

docs.title: 8. Compliance Mapping
docs.text:
The Management Plane provides evidence for:

  • CM-2, CM-3, CM-6, CM-7, CM-8
  • IR-4, IR-5, IR-6, IR-8
  • AC-19
  • IA-3
  • CA-7
  • SC-28

It also generates KSIs:

  • KSI-CMT (baseline drift)
  • KSI-PIY (inventory)
  • KSI-IAM (identity/device trust)
  • KSI-MLA (telemetry)

  • CDM/CLAW integration

docs.title: Program Vision
docs.text: Field Value Version 1.0 Date 2026-03 Classification CUI/FOUO Source Plane(s) Identity, Network, Addressing, Telemetry, Security, Management Document Type Leadership Vision (01_Canon)

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document defines the Program Vision for the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay (UIAO) modernization initiative. It articulates the strategic intent, mission alignment, guiding principles, and end-state outcomes required to transform the agency into a cloud-optimized, identity-driven, telemetry-informed federal enterprise aligned with Zero Trust, TIC 3.0, and FedRAMP 20x.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Strategic modernization vision
  • Mission alignment and business drivers
  • Guiding principles
  • End-state architectural outcomes
  • Technology pillars supporting the program

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Detailed architecture (see foundational documents)
  • Compliance mappings (see FedRAMP crosswalk)
  • Project sequencing (see modernization timeline)

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
The Program Vision spans all six UIAO control planes:

Plane Strategic Role Identity Foundation of Zero Trust and access governance Network Cloud-first routing and segmentation Addressing Deterministic IPAM and location intelligence Telemetry & Location Real-time truth source for decisions Security & Compliance Alignment with TIC 3.0, FedRAMP 20x, NIST 800-63 Management Governance, drift detection, and continuous authorization

The vision describes how these planes unify into a single modernization program.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text:
The Program Vision is anchored in the Seven Core Concepts of UIAO:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit
  2. Identity as the root namespace
  3. Deterministic addressing
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay
  5. Telemetry as control
  6. Embedded governance & automation
  7. Public service first

These concepts define the modernization philosophy and guide all program decisions.

docs.title: 6.1 Purpose of the Modernization Program
docs.text:
The agency must modernize to:

  • Improve mission readiness
  • Reduce cyber risk
  • Meet federal mandates
  • Improve citizen-facing services
  • Replace legacy perimeter architectures
  • Enable cloud-first operations
  • Support real-time telemetry and location services

UIAO provides the architectural foundation for this transformation.

docs.title: 6.2 Technology Pillars
docs.text:
The modernization program is built on real, production-grade federal technologies:

  • Microsoft Entra ID — Identity Control Plane
  • ICAM (NIST 800-63, OMB M-19-17) — Governance backbone
  • InfoBlox IPAM — Authoritative Addressing Control Plane
  • Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN — Network Control Plane
  • Cloud Telemetry + Location Services — Telemetry Control Plane
  • TIC 3.0 Cloud + Branch — Security & Compliance Plane

These pillars form the operational substrate of UIAO.

docs.title: 6.3 Guiding Principles
docs.text:
The modernization program is governed by the following principles:

  • Zero Trust by default
  • Identity as the new perimeter
  • Telemetry as the truth source
  • Cloud-first routing
  • Incremental modernization (no big-bang)
  • FedRAMP-aligned controls
  • Modular, extensible architecture

These principles ensure modernization is sustainable, compliant, and mission-aligned.

docs.title: 6.4 End-State Vision
docs.text:
The end state is a fully modernized, identity-driven, cloud-optimized, telemetry-rich federal network where:

  • Identity governs access, addressing, certificates, and policy
  • Routing is cloud-first and performance-optimized
  • Addressing is deterministic and identity-derived
  • Telemetry provides real-time assurance and location inference
  • Governance is automated and drift-resistant
  • Security is continuous and Zero Trust-aligned
  • Citizen experience is prioritized in every decision

This is the target architecture for the agency.

docs.title: 7. Runtime Model
docs.text: The Program Vision adopts the UIAO runtime model:

docs.title: 7.1 Conversation-Centric Operation
docs.text:
Every interaction is treated as a conversation with:

  • Identity
  • Addressing
  • Certificates
  • Path
  • QoS
  • Telemetry

bound together as a single correlated unit.

docs.title: 7.2 Deterministic Behavior
docs.text: Given identical identity, boundary, telemetry, and assurance inputs, the system produces identical decisions across clouds and agencies.

docs.title: 7.3 Continuous Evaluation
docs.text:
Telemetry continuously informs:

  • Routing
  • Access
  • Segmentation
  • Compliance posture

This ensures real-time alignment with Zero Trust and FedRAMP 20x.

docs.title: 8. Compliance Mapping
docs.text: The Program Vision aligns with:

docs.title: 8.1 FedRAMP 20x
docs.text:
• Class C (Moderate)
  • Telemetry-based validation
  • OSCAL machine-readability
  • Automated evidence generation

docs.title: 8.2 TIC 3.0
docs.text:
• Cloud Use Case
  • Branch Use Case

docs.title: 1. Title Page
docs.text: Field Value Format Executive Slide-Deck (Markdown) Version 1.0 Date March 2026 Classification CUI/FOUO Source Planes Identity, Network, Addressing, Telemetry, Security, Management Document Type Leadership Briefing (01_Canon)

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document provides an executive-level briefing on the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay Architecture (UIAO). It is designed for CIOs, CISOs, CTOs, PMOs, and mission executives who need to understand the modernization program at a glance. The briefing is structured as a slide-deck narrative and covers program overview, challenges, vision, architecture, compliance alignment, governance, timeline, and outcomes.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Program overview and strategic goal
  • Current state challenges and mission impact
  • End-state vision and guiding principles
  • Six Control Planes and Seven Core Concepts
  • Frozen state analysis
  • Program outcomes
  • Runtime model
  • Compliance alignment (FedRAMP 20x, TIC 3.0, NIST 800-63)
  • Governance and drift controls
  • Modernization timeline summary
  • Executive summary

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Detailed technical implementation procedures
  • Vendor procurement or licensing details
  • Operational runbooks or playbooks
  • Per-device or per-endpoint configuration

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text: Plane Strategic Role Identity Entra ID, ICAM governance, Conditional Access, PIM, lifecycle automation Network Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, Cloud OnRamp for M365, INR integration, identity-aware segmentation Addressing InfoBlox IPAM, DNS/DHCP modernization, cloud IPAM reconciliation Telemetry M365 Network Telemetry, SD-WAN telemetry, endpoint telemetry, DNS telemetry, CDM/CLAW, SIEM ingestion Security TIC 3.0 Cloud + Branch, Zero Trust, FedRAMP alignment, NIST 800-63, ICAM governance Management ServiceNow (CMDB, change, drift detection), Intune (device trust, compliance, configuration)

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text:
The Seven Core Concepts govern the UIAO architecture:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit — Every interaction binds identity, certificates, addressing, path, QoS, and telemetry.
  2. Identity as the root namespace — Every IP, certificate, subnet, policy, and telemetry event is derived from identity.
  3. Deterministic addressing — Addressing is identity-derived and policy-driven.
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay — mTLS anchors tunnels, services, and trust relationships.
  5. Telemetry as control — Telemetry is a real-time control input, not passive reporting.
  6. Embedded governance and automation — Governance is executed through orchestrated workflows, not manual tickets.
  7. Public service first — Citizen experience, accessibility, and privacy are top-level design constraints.

docs.title: Slide 1 — Program Overview
docs.text:
Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay Architecture (UIAO) is a coordinated modernization effort across:

  • Microsoft Entra ID — Identity Control Plane
  • ICAM (NIST 800-63, OMB M-19-17) — Governance Backbone
  • InfoBlox IPAM — Addressing Control Plane
  • Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN — Network Control Plane
  • Cloud Telemetry + Location Services — Telemetry Control Plane
  • TIC 3.0 Cloud + Branch — Security and Compliance Plane

Strategic Goal: Transform the agency into a cloud-optimized, identity-driven, telemetry-informed, Zero Trust-aligned, TIC 3.0-compliant federal enterprise.

docs.title: Current State Challenges
docs.text:
• TIC 2.0 hairpin bottlenecks
  • AD-centric identity with limited governance
  • Fragmented IPAM across cloud and on-prem
  • Limited telemetry visibility
  • No INR or E911 readiness
  • Inconsistent Zero Trust enforcement

docs.title: Mission Impact
docs.text:
• Poor M365 performance
  • Increased cyber risk
  • Compliance gaps (TIC 3.0, FedRAMP 20x, SCuBA)
  • Operational inefficiencies
  • Slower mission execution

docs.title: Slide 3 — Program Vision
docs.text:
End State: A fully modernized, identity-driven, cloud-optimized, telemetry-rich federal network.

Guiding Principles:

  • Zero Trust by default
  • Identity as the new perimeter
  • Telemetry as the truth source
  • Cloud-first routing
  • Incremental modernization (no big-bang)
  • FedRAMP-aligned controls
  • Modular, extensible architecture

Design Principle: If it degrades the citizen interaction, it does not ship.

docs.title: Identity Control Plane
docs.text:
• Entra ID
  • ICAM governance
  • Conditional Access
  • PIM
  • Lifecycle automation

docs.title: Network Control Plane
docs.text:
• Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN
  • Cloud OnRamp for M365
  • INR integration
  • Identity-aware segmentation

docs.title: Addressing Control Plane
docs.text:
• InfoBlox IPAM
  • DNS/DHCP modernization
  • Cloud IPAM reconciliation

docs.title: Telemetry and Location Control Plane
docs.text:
• M365 Network Telemetry
  • SD-WAN telemetry
  • Endpoint telemetry (Defender/Intune)
  • DNS telemetry
  • CDM/CLAW reporting
  • SIEM ingestion

docs.title: Security and Compliance Plane
docs.text:
• TIC 3.0 Cloud + Branch
  • Zero Trust
  • FedRAMP alignment
  • NIST 800-63
  • ICAM governance

docs.title: Management Plane
docs.text:
• ServiceNow (CMDB, change, drift detection)
  • Intune (device trust, compliance, configuration)

docs.title: Slide 5 — Frozen State Analysis
docs.text: Domain Current State Gap Identity On-prem AD, siloed No unified identity graph Addressing Static spreadsheets No identity-to-address binding Network Security L3/L4 firewalls No identity-aware segmentation Endpoint Mixed tooling No unified posture signal App Delivery Local auth No workload identity Telemetry Siloed logs No conversation-level correlation Governance Email/tickets No automated enforcement Data Protection Manual classification No data-aware routing

docs.title: Slide 6 — Program Outcomes
docs.text:
• Reduced latency and improved M365 performance
  • Stronger identity governance and compliance
  • Accurate addressing and location inference
  • Real-time telemetry for routing and security
  • TIC 3.0 compliance across cloud and branch
  • A unified, future-proof modernization foundation

docs.title: Conversation Flow
docs.text:
1. Identity initiates
  2. Addressing binds
  3. Certificates authenticate
  4. Overlay establishes path
  5. Telemetry validates
  6. Policy evaluates continuously

docs.title: Determinism
docs.text: Given identical identity, boundary, telemetry, and assurance inputs, the system produces identical decisions across clouds and agencies.

docs.title: Continuous Evaluation
docs.text: Telemetry continuously informs routing, access, segmentation, and compliance posture.

docs.title: FedRAMP 20x
docs.text:
• Class C (Moderate)
  • Telemetry-based validation
  • OSCAL machine-readability
  • Automated evidence generation

docs.title: TIC 3.0
docs.text:
• Cloud Use Case
  • Branch Use Case
  • Identity-centric segmentation

  • ICAM governance

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
The modernization timeline spans all six UIAO control planes:

Plane Timeline Role Identity First modernization priority; foundation for all others Network Cloud-first routing, segmentation, DIA, INR Addressing Deterministic IPAM, DNS/DHCP modernization Telemetry and Location Real-time signals for routing, security, compliance Security and Compliance TIC 3.0, Zero Trust, FedRAMP 20x Management Drift detection, CMDB, device compliance

The timeline ensures each plane is modernized in the correct order.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts

The modernization timeline operationalizes the Seven Core Concepts:

  1. Conversation as the atomic unit
  2. Identity as the root namespace
  3. Deterministic addressing
  4. Certificate-anchored overlay
  5. Telemetry as control
  6. Embedded governance and automation
  7. Public service first

These concepts guide sequencing and prioritization.

docs.title: 6.1 Workstream Overview
docs.text: The modernization program is divided into four coordinated workstreams:

docs.text:

  • MFA/SSPR

  • PIM
  • Device trust

docs.title: Workstream B — Network Modernization

docs.title: Workstream C — Addressing Modernization

docs.title: Workstream D — Telemetry and Governance

  • Endpoint telemetry
  • ServiceNow CMDB
  • Intune compliance
  • TIC 3.0 Cloud + Branch

These workstreams operate in parallel but with strict dependency ordering.

  • Addressing modernization
  • Network segmentation
  • Telemetry correlation
  • Zero Trust enforcement

docs.text: Telemetry integration begins early and matures throughout the timeline.

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document defines the authoritative directory structure for the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay (UIAO) repository. It establishes the canonical layout, naming conventions, numbering rules, and document placement required to maintain a deterministic, drift-resistant, self-navigating modernization canon.

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts

  6. Embedded governance and automation — Structure supports automation
  7. Public service first — Clear, accessible, predictable layout

   08_ModernizationTimeline.md

   09_CrosswalkIndex.md
   10_DirectoryStructure.md
   11_GlossaryAndDefinitions.md

This structure is deterministic and must not be altered.

docs.title: 6.2 Numbering Rules
docs.text:
Rule 1 — Two-Digit Prefix

Every document begins with a two-digit prefix:

  • 00-02: Foundational Architecture
  • 03-05: Compliance and Governance
  • 06-08: Program and Leadership
  • 09-11: Index and Metadata

Rule 2 — No Gaps

Numbers must remain contiguous. If a document is removed, its number is retired permanently.

Rule 3 — No Renumbering

Once assigned, a number is immutable.

Rule 4 — Appendices Use Suffixes

Appendices follow the pattern:

  • A_*.md
  • B_*.md
  • C_*.md

Rule 5 — No Nested Directories for Canon Documents

All 12 canonical documents live directly under /docs.

docs.title: 7. Runtime Model
docs.text: The directory structure mirrors the UIAO runtime model:

docs.title: 7.1 Conversation Flow to Document Flow
docs.text:
Identity - Addressing - Certificates - Overlay - Telemetry - Policy

maps to

00 - 01 - 02 - 03 - 04 - 05 - 06 - 07 - 08 - 09 - 10 - 11

docs.title: 7.2 Deterministic Navigation
docs.text: Given the number, the document's role is always known.

docs.title: 7.3 Continuous Evaluation
docs.text: Crosswalks and governance documents (03-05, 09-11) support continuous monitoring.

docs.text: Field Value Version 1.0 Date March 2026 Classification CUI/FOUO Source Planes Identity, Network, Addressing, Telemetry, Security, Management Document Type Canon Glossary (02_Appendices)

docs.title: 2. Purpose
docs.text: This document provides the authoritative glossary for the Unified Identity-Addressing-Overlay (UIAO) canon. It defines all core terms, architectural concepts, control-plane vocabulary, compliance terminology, and modernization language used throughout the 12-document corpus. These definitions are frozen and must not be altered without a formal canon revision.

docs.title: Included
docs.text:
• Canonical definitions of all UIAO terms
  • Control-plane terminology
  • Compliance and governance vocabulary
  • Acronyms and abbreviations
  • Cross-plane conceptual language

docs.title: Excluded
docs.text:
• Vendor-specific product documentation
  • Implementation-specific terminology
  • Non-canonical slang or shorthand

docs.title: 4. Control Plane Alignment
docs.text:
This glossary spans all six UIAO control planes:

Plane Vocabulary Domain Identity Identity, assurance, lifecycle, governance Network Routing, segmentation, overlay, DIA, INR Addressing IPAM, DNS/DHCP, deterministic addressing Telemetry and Location Telemetry, location inference, KSI Security and Compliance Zero Trust, TIC 3.0, FedRAMP Management CMDB, drift detection, configuration baselines

docs.title: 5. Core Concepts
docs.text: The glossary includes the frozen definitions of the Seven Core Concepts:

docs.title: Conversation as the Atomic Unit
docs.text: A correlated interaction consisting of identity, addressing, certificates, path, QoS, and telemetry.

docs.title: Identity as the Root Namespace
docs.text: Identity governs addressing, certificates, segmentation, and policy.

docs.title: Deterministic Addressing
docs.text: IP addresses derived from identity attributes and policy, not spreadsheets or manual assignment.

docs.title: Certificate-Anchored Overlay
docs.text: mTLS-anchored tunnels and service chains that enforce identity-aware routing.

docs.title: Telemetry as Control
docs.text: Telemetry is a real-time control input for routing, access, segmentation, and compliance.

docs.title: Embedded Governance and Automation
docs.text: Governance executed through automated workflows, not manual tickets.

docs.title: Public Service First
docs.text: Citizen experience, accessibility, and privacy are top-level design constraints.

docs.title: 6. Glossary (Canonical Definitions)
docs.text: Below is the authoritative glossary for the UIAO canon.

docs.title: A
docs.text:
AC-4 (Access Control) NIST control governing segmentation and identity-aware routing.

Addressing Control Plane The plane responsible for deterministic IPAM, DNS/DHCP, and identity-derived addressing.

Asset Inventory (CM-8) Authoritative list of hardware, software, and virtual assets maintained through CMDB and IPAM.

docs.title: B
docs.text:
Baseline Configuration (CM-2) Approved configuration settings enforced through Intune and validated through CMDB.

Branch Modernization TIC 3.0 Branch Use Case implementation using SD-WAN and identity-aware segmentation.

docs.title: C
docs.text:
CA-7 (Continuous Monitoring) NIST control requiring real-time telemetry and automated evidence generation.

Certificate-Anchored Overlay mTLS-anchored tunnels that enforce identity-aware routing and segmentation.

CMDB (Configuration Management Database) Authoritative system of record for assets, configurations, and relationships.

Conditional Access Identity-driven access control based on device posture, location, and assurance.

Conversation The atomic unit of UIAO runtime behavior.

Crosswalk A mapping between UIAO concepts and external compliance frameworks.

docs.title: D
docs.text:
Deterministic Addressing Identity-derived IP addressing that eliminates manual assignment and spreadsheet drift.

DIA (Direct Internet Access) Cloud-first routing model that bypasses legacy TIC 2.0 bottlenecks.

docs.title: E
docs.text:
E911 Emergency location services requiring accurate, real-time location inference.

Entra ID Authoritative identity provider for UIAO.

docs.title: F
docs.text:
FedRAMP 20x Telemetry-based validation framework for federal cloud services.

Frozen State Legacy architectural constraints that prevent modernization.

docs.title: G
docs.text: Governance Automated enforcement of identity, configuration, and compliance policies.

docs.title: H
docs.text: HLD (High-Level Design) Architecture-level design documents defining system behavior and boundaries.

docs.title: I
docs.text:
Identity Assurance Confidence level in the authenticity of an identity, governed by NIST 800-63.

Identity Control Plane The plane responsible for identity lifecycle, authentication, and assurance.

INR (Intelligent Network Routing) Location-aware routing based on telemetry and identity.

IPAM (IP Address Management) Authoritative addressing system for deterministic IP allocation.

docs.title: K
docs.text: KSI (Key Security Indicator) Telemetry-based evidence required for FedRAMP 20x validation.

docs.title: L
docs.text:
LLD (Low-Level Design) Implementation-level design documents defining configuration details.

Location Inference Determining user or device location based on IPAM, SD-WAN, and telemetry.

docs.title: M
docs.text:
Management Plane The plane responsible for CMDB, drift detection, device compliance, and governance.

mTLS (Mutual TLS) Certificate-based authentication used for overlay tunnels.

docs.title: N
docs.text:
NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Primary federal security control framework.

NIST SP 800-63 Identity assurance and authentication framework.

docs.title: O
docs.text: Overlay Identity-anchored, certificate-secured virtual network layer.

docs.title: P
docs.text:
PIM (Privileged Identity Management) Governance system for privileged access.

Public Service First Design principle prioritizing citizen experience and accessibility.

docs.title: R
docs.text: Runtime Model UIAO's operational model based on conversation-level evaluation.

docs.title: S
docs.text:
SCuBA CISA's M365 hardening and identity governance framework.

SD-WAN Software-defined WAN used for cloud-first routing and segmentation.

Segmentation Identity-aware network separation enforced through SD-WAN.

ServiceNow Authoritative system for CMDB, change control, and drift detection.

docs.title: T
docs.text:
Telemetry Real-time signals used for routing, access, segmentation, and compliance.

TIC 3.0 Modern federal perimeter and cloud security framework.

docs.title: Z
docs.text: Zero Trust Security model requiring continuous verification of identity, device, and context.

docs.title: UIAO AI Security Principles
docs.text: Version 1.0

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document establishes security principles for AI integration within the UIAO architecture. AI expands the attack surface by introducing new data flows between production systems and models. These principles ensure AI accelerates modernization without becoming a vector for data exfiltration.

docs.title: Core Risk: AI Leakage
docs.text: AI Leakage occurs when sensitive artifacts or derived knowledge are exposed through AI systems, model training, prompts, logs, connectors, or downstream outputs. This includes unauthorized disclosure of code, business logic, PII, credentials, or operational runbooks.

docs.title: Impact and Likelihood
docs.text: Impact Likelihood Primary Consequences High Medium-High IP loss, regulatory fines, operational disruption, reputational damage

docs.title: Security Principles
docs.text: Principle Purpose Priority Data Minimization Reduce exposure surface for models and connectors Critical Private Inference Boundaries Prevent uncontrolled egress to public LLMs Critical Provenance and Auditability Enable forensic review and regulatory proof High Least Privilege and Scoped Connectors Limit what AI can access or modify High Human-in-the-Loop Governance Ensure judgment for high-risk decisions Critical Secrets Hygiene Prevent credential leakage via prompts or repos High Safe Training Practices Prevent model memorization of sensitive data Medium Continuous Threat Validation Detect evolving exfiltration techniques Medium Legal and Data Residency Compliance Avoid cross-border and regulatory violations Critical Operational Resilience and Rollback Limit blast radius of AI-driven changes High

docs.title: Expanded Data Risk Catalog
docs.text: Risk What Can Be Exposed Why It Matters PII Leakage Names, SSNs, health, payment data Models can memorize and reproduce training data IP / Source Code Proprietary algorithms, business logic Public model use can surface proprietary code Credential Exfiltration API keys, DB passwords, tokens Notebooks and prompts often contain secrets Operational Runbooks Incident response steps, escalation paths Reveals how to disrupt or manipulate systems Telemetry and Logs Job traces, performance metrics Runtime context reveals system weaknesses Model Memorization Verbatim records from training corpora LLMs can reproduce memorized fragments Context-Memory Leakage Prior conversations, cached prompts Persistent memory can expose earlier inputs Plugin/Connector Leaks Third-party integration data Connectors can return sensitive content Cross-Border Compliance Data moved across jurisdictions Violates data residency and privacy laws Derivative Reconstruction Rebuilding system behavior from outputs Attackers infer business rules from responses

docs.title: Required Controls
docs.text:
1. Model and Environment Isolation: Use private or on-prem inference in VPC or air-gapped environments
  2. Deterministic Extraction and Redaction: Extract structured metadata and redact secrets before any AI ingestion
  3. Secrets Management and Scanning: Enforce secret scanning in CI/CD and rotate keys on detection
  4. Data Minimization for Training: Never fine-tune public models on raw production artifacts
  5. Access Control and Least Privilege: Limit who can query models and what data they can submit
  6. Provenance, Logging, and Audit: Log every AI input/output, model version, and human approval
  7. Human-in-the-Loop Gating: Require SME sign-off before applying AI-suggested changes to production
  8. Legal and Compliance Review: Assess regulatory implications before ingesting PII or regulated data
  9. Continuous Monitoring and Red-Team Testing: Simulate leakage scenarios quarterly

docs.title: Acceptance Criteria for AI Projects
docs.text:
• All inputs are scanned and redacted for secrets and PII
  • Models run in isolated, auditable environments
  • Every AI suggestion has an auditable human approval before production change
  • Provenance records exist for training data, prompts, and outputs
  • No raw production credentials or unredacted logs used for model training
  • Red-team test passed for the project's data class

docs.title: KPIs and Monitoring
docs.text:
• Percentage of AI workloads in private environments (target: 100% for sensitive classes)
  • Mean time to detect and rotate exposed secrets (target: <24 hours)
  • Number of AI outputs requiring SME correction (trend should decline)
  • Red-team leakage test failure rate (target: 0%)
  • Percentage of AI ingestions with full provenance (target: 100%)

docs.title: Mainframe Modernization Context
docs.text:
AI can substantially augment COBOL/Fortran modernization tasks (analysis, translation, testing, documentation) but cannot fully replace experienced mainframe engineers for complex, mission-critical systems. A hybrid approach (AI + senior domain experts) is the practical path:

  • Discovery + AI Analysis: Map code, dependencies, and produce human-readable summaries
  • Selective Automated Refactor: Use AI for pure business logic with low operational coupling
  • Human Review and Redesign: Senior engineers validate non-functional behavior and sign off tests
  • Iterative Testing: AI-generated tests plus manual scenario testing before cutover

End of UIAO AI Security Principles v1.0

docs.title: UIAO FIMF Adapter Registry and Federated Truth Fabric
docs.text: Version 1.0

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document establishes the FIMF (Federated Identity Management Framework) adapter registry for UIAO and defines the pointer-first, claims-not-copies architecture that preserves truth and prevents data bloat across federal, state, and local jurisdictions.

docs.title: Core Architectural Principle
docs.text: Store the minimal canonical claim and immutable provenance; otherwise store a secure pointer and fetch signed assertions on demand. Raw authoritative records remain at source.

docs.title: Adapter Registry (Priority Order)
docs.text: Adapter Connects To Claim Type Transport Confidence Mainframe (z/OS Connect / MQ) Legacy mainframes Mapped from COBOL records z/OS Connect, MQ, batch High SSA SSN Social Security systems ssn:identity Mutual TLS API, signed XML/JSON High State DMV (MD MVA) State DMV systems dmv:license State API, batch, MQ bridge High IRS / ITIN IRS taxpayer identity itin:identity Mutual TLS API, secure file transfer High DHS / USCIS Immigration Immigration status immigration:status Mutual TLS API, secure MQ High DoS Passport Passport records passport:identity API or batch, signed assertions High DoD / Military ID DoD identity stores military:id Secure DoD gateway, mutual TLS High State Vital Records Birth/death registries vital:event Secure API, event feed High OPM / HRIS Federal HR systems agency:employee HR API, SFTP, SCIM High Payments / Treasury Payment systems payment:status Secure API, batch High PKI / Certificate Authority PIV, certificates credential:cert OCSP, CRL, PKI APIs High IdP / SSO (SAML/OIDC) Identity providers Signed assertions with xALs SAML, OIDC/OAuth Varies CMDB / Asset Management Agency CMDBs asset:endpoint REST API, telemetry bus Medium-High Agency Case Management Program case systems case:status REST API, event bus Medium IoT / Telemetry Sensors, endpoints Aggregated pointers MQTT, time-series DB Low-Medium Consent Management Consent registries consent:token API, signed tokens High Purpose Token Issuer Legal/policy authority purpose:token OAuth2/JWT issuance High Adjudication Workflow Human review systems adjudication:task Task API, case queue Human-in-loop Audit / SIEM Security logs audit:event Syslog, SIEM APIs High

docs.title: What UIAO Stores vs Points To
docs.text: Pattern UIAO Behavior Rationale Authoritative fact, low latency Store canonical claim + provenance; keep pointer to source Fast reads with provenance Rare or sensitive fact Do not store; return pointer + signed assertion on demand Minimizes risk and legal exposure High-volume telemetry Store aggregates and pointers; raw telemetry in time-series stores Prevents graph bloat Temporary session data Cache ephemeral claims with strict TTL Improves UX without creating truth copies Emergency offline needs Store encrypted snapshot with legal justification and short retention Operational necessity with governance

docs.title: Canonical Claim Structure
docs.text:
Every adapter must emit:

  • Canonical Claim: claim_id, claim_type, hashed_identifier, value (minimal), confidence_score, recency_ts
  • Provenance Record: source_authority, source_record_id, signed_digest, signature_ts, adapter_version, legal_basis
  • Pointer/Locator: source_endpoint_uri, access_method (API/batch), access_token_policy
  • Access Audit: requester_id, purpose_token, response_minimization, audit_ts

docs.title: Adapter Onboarding Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Signed MOU and legal basis for sharing
  • [ ] Federation Practice Statement published
  • [ ] Schema mapping to canonical claim
  • [ ] Signed assertion format and key exchange
  • [ ] Transport and ATO (mutual TLS, FIPS/NIST controls)
  • [ ] Provenance fields validated on ingest
  • [ ] Confidence scoring rules defined
  • [ ] TTL and retention policy set
  • [ ] Acceptance tests: signature validation, provenance completeness, purpose token enforcement, performance SLA

docs.title: Anti-Bloat Controls
docs.text:
• Claims not copies enforced by adapter onboarding checklist
  • Pointer-first default; caching only with explicit SLA and TTL
  • Provenance mandatory for every claim
  • Human adjudication gate for conflicts or confidence below threshold
  • Pruning jobs scheduled for stale derived artifacts
  • Separate telemetry from truth: high-volume data stays in time-series stores

docs.title: Confidence Scoring Rules
docs.text: Level Criteria Allowed Actions High Signed real-time API from authoritative source, recency <30 days Automated decisions; cache minimal claim Medium Batch extract with checksum, recency <180 days Human review for critical decisions Low Probabilistic match, scanned docs, partial identifiers Never automated; route to adjudication

docs.title: FIMF Governance Structure
docs.text:
• Federated Council: Executive approvals, trust agreements, dispute resolution
  • FIMF Working Group: Technical standards, adapter certification, metadata registry
  • Privacy Board: Independent review of purpose tokens, minimization, and audits

docs.title: KPIs
docs.text:
• Claim Duplication Factor (target: <=1.5)
  • Provenance Completeness (target: 100%)
  • Adjudication Volume (target: trending down)
  • Shadow DB Count (target: quarterly reduction)
  • Cache TTL Compliance (target: 100%)
  • False Automation Rate (target: below agreed threshold)

End of UIAO FIMF Adapter Registry v1.0

docs.title: TIC 3.0 Transition and F5 Proxy Retirement Roadmap
docs.text: Source: Copilot thread "Phasing Out F5 Proxies in a TIC 3.0 World" Phase: Phase 4 — Network Modernization Status: Canonical Reference

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text: Outbound F5 proxies are a TIC 2.0 artifact incompatible with M365, multi-cloud, and UIAO architecture. TIC 3.0 Cloud Use Case eliminates the need for centralized egress chokepoints, replacing them with identity-first, telemetry-driven, direct-to-cloud access patterns.

docs.title: Why Outbound F5 Proxies Must Be Retired
Architectural Incompatibility
docs.text: Legacy Pattern (TIC 2.0) Modern Pattern (UIAO/TIC 3.0) Centralized egress chokepoints Direct-to-service access TLS interception End-to-end TLS, cert pinning, ECH Perimeter-based trust Identity-first trust Static routing Dynamic, cloud-aware pathing Boundary control Continuous trust evaluation Inspection at choke Attestation and telemetry

docs.title: M365 Services Broken by Outbound Proxies
docs.text:
• Teams media pathing
  • Informed Network Routing (INR)
  • E911 location services
  • Telemetry collection
  • Conditional Access signals
  • Copilot performance
  • SharePoint/Exchange latency
  • TLS service classification

docs.title: UIAO-Aligned Replacement Stack
docs.text: Component Purpose SD-WAN with local DIA breakout M365 traffic breaks out locally, uninspected Entra ID + Conditional Access Identity-first policy enforcement Defender for Endpoint/Cloud Apps Cloud-native security controls SASE/ZTNA (Zscaler, Netskope, Prisma) Non-M365 traffic security DNS-layer security (Infoblox, BlueCat) DNS resolution and threat protection No TLS interception for M365 Non-negotiable requirement

docs.title: Where F5 Remains Valid
docs.text: Valid Role Invalid Role Inbound application delivery (BIG-IP, NGINX) Outbound proxy API gateways TLS interception for SaaS WAF for custom apps M365 traffic inspection Internal load balancing (transitional) Centralized egress chokepoint Distributed Cloud (XC) for edge —

docs.title: Internal Load Balancing Transition
docs.text: F5 remains needed for internal LB only for legacy workloads that cannot yet be containerized or moved to cloud-native LB. This role is transitional, not strategic.

docs.title: Retirement Phases
docs.text: Phase F5 Role Cloud-Native Equivalent Phase 1 — Today Internal LB for legacy workloads — Phase 2 — Transition Apps move to Azure App Service, AWS ECS/EKS, K8s Azure Front Door, App Gateway, AWS ALB/NLB Phase 3 — UIAO Steady State Minimal: handful of legacy systems only Cloud-native LB dominates

docs.title: Core Identity Shift
docs.text: From: "We manage the F5s." To: "We engineer the agency's application delivery and access fabric."

docs.title: Skills Translation Map
docs.text: Legacy Skill Future Value VIPs, pools, health checks Cloud LB (ALB/NLB, App Gateway, Front Door) TLS termination, cert chains Zero Trust, identity-bound TLS, service mesh mTLS iRules, traffic steering API gateways, routing policies, mesh traffic shaping Troubleshooting flows Multi-cloud observability, distributed tracing Understanding app quirks App modernization mapping and dependency analysis High-stakes outage response Cloud incident response and SRE patterns

docs.title: Four Future Roles for F5 Engineers
docs.text:
1. Cloud Load Balancing Engineer — Azure Front Door, App Gateway, AWS ALB/NLB
  2. API Gateway & Service Mesh Engineer — APIM, Kong, Istio, Linkerd, Envoy
  3. Zero Trust Access & Traffic Policy Engineer — Conditional Access, SASE/ZTNA
  4. Application Modernization Flow Architect — Dependency mapping, cutover design

docs.title: 90-Day Transition Playbook
docs.text: Phase Weeks Activities Reframe 1-2 Communicate new mission; show skills mapping; remove fear Upskill 3-8 Azure Front Door, AWS ALB, APIM, Istio/Envoy, Conditional Access, SD-WAN, cloud networking Shadow & Swap 9-12 Own cloud LB configs, write API gateway policies, replace F5 VIPs with cloud-native equivalents

docs.title: Governance Replacement Pattern
docs.text:
Outbound proxies provided an illusion of control. UIAO replaces this with:

Old Model New Model Boundary control Identity control Chokepoints Telemetry Inspection Attestation Perimeter trust Continuous trust evaluation Centralized enforcement Federated governance

docs.title: Federal Workforce Modernization Context
docs.text: GAO and CIO.gov reports consistently identify workforce skills gaps as a primary barrier to modernization across all federal agencies. Modernization documents should explicitly recommend repurposing personnel from obsolete technologies into cloud, Zero Trust, and application delivery roles.

docs.title: Key Federal Findings
docs.text:
• GAO 2025: 11 of most critical systems use outdated languages; 7 have known cybersecurity vulnerabilities
  • CIO.gov: Workforce skills are a primary barrier to Zero Trust and cloud adoption
  • VA lost 12% of IT workforce in one year; relied on cross-training and upskilling

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• docs/01_UnifiedArchitecture.md — UIAO identity-first architecture
  • docs/08_ModernizationTimeline.md — Phase sequencing
  • docs/03_FedRAMP20x_Crosswalk.md — TIC 3.0 alignment
  • docs/12_AI_SecurityPrinciples.md — AI security controls

docs.title: Copilot Integration Log for uiao-core
docs.text: Generated: 2026-03-31 Source: Microsoft Copilot Pro conversation threads Purpose: Digest of UIAO architectural decisions, patterns, and artifacts from Copilot sessions for integration into uiao-core

docs.title: Thread 1: Creating a Dev Repo for UIAO Core
Key Decisions
docs.text:
• uiao-core-dev recommended as temporary staging repo for experimentation, compiler integration, and docs evolution
  • One-Way Canonical Sync (Model 1) selected: uiao-core -> uiao-core-dev only; promotion back is intentional, not automatic
  • Two-way sync rejected (drift risk); long-running feature branch model noted as acceptable alternative

docs.title: Promotion Workflow (Dev -> Core)
docs.text:
1. Prepare promotion candidate in uiao-core-dev
  2. Run drift checks, schema validation, compiler validation, governance invariants
  3. Create promote-YYYYMMDD branch
  4. Sync with core via git fetch core && git rebase core/main
  5. Open PR from uiao-core-dev -> uiao-core with validation results
  6. Human approval required
  7. Merge into canon; dev pulls from core to reset drift

docs.title: Branching Model
docs.text: uiao-core: main only (no feature branches, no drafts) uiao-core-dev: main, feature/*, structure/*, compiler/*, migration/*, promote/*

docs.title: GitHub Actions for Drift-Resistance
docs.text: Action Scope Purpose Drift Check Both repos Validates directory structure against canonical schema Schema Validation Both repos Validates YAML/JSON against schemas Compiler Validation Both repos Ensures documents compile cleanly Promotion Gate Core only Blocks PRs unless all checks pass Sync Reminder Dev only Weekly alert if dev is behind core by N commits

docs.title: Integration Target
docs.text:
• docs/adr/ - Architecture Decision Record for dev repo strategy
  • .github/workflows/ - Drift check and promotion gate actions
  • CONTRIBUTING.md - Update with promotion workflow

docs.title: UIAO Integration Suite Structure
docs.text:
Four-part canonical document set generated:

Part Content I - Developer Integration Checklist Environment, identity, network, API, ops, security, testing requirements II - Canonical API Specification Base URL versioning, auth (OAuth2/JWT), endpoints, error schema, performance III - Build Your Own UIAO-Compatible System Reference architecture, capability design, execution engine, task manager IV - Architecture Pattern Library Gateway, execution engine, capability registry, task manager, observability, security envelope

docs.title: Required UIAO-Compatible Endpoints
docs.text:
• GET /health - System health
  • GET /capabilities - Supported actions
  • POST /execute - Initiate action
  • GET /status/{taskId} - Task state

docs.title: Entra ID Integration (Both Directions)
docs.text:
Direction A (UIAO -> Entra ID): UIAO authenticates to Entra-protected APIs using client credentials. Use cases: user provisioning via Graph, case management API calls, compliance checks.

Direction B (Entra ID -> UIAO): UIAO validates Entra-issued tokens as a protected API. Use cases: internal portal automation, scheduled batch tasks, mobile field technician actions.

docs.title: HA Architecture for GitHub-Based UIAO
docs.text:
• Control Plane: GitHub (primary) + mirror (passive)
  • Execution Plane: Multi-region self-hosted runners (active-active)
  • Data Plane: Cosmos DB multi-region writes, Event Hub, Blob GRS, Key Vault geo-replicated
  • Service Plane: Downstream APIs in two regions behind global load balancer
  • Workflow Plane: Stateless, idempotent, region-aware, retry-safe

docs.title: Integration Target
docs.text:
• docs/ - New 11_IntegrationSuite.md or split into sub-docs
  • schemas/ - API contract schemas (health, capabilities, execute, status)
  • data/overlay-config.yml - Entra ID integration config patterns
  • canon/ - UIAO Integration Suite as canonical reference

docs.title: Thread 3: UIAO and Template ATO Integration
Key Finding
docs.text: Template ATOs exist federally (GSA LATO, CMS RMF, Sprint ATO) but cannot be imported directly. UIAO can canonically absorb them via a UIAO ATO Overlay Pack.

docs.title: UIAO ATO Overlay Pack (FedRAMP 20x Phase 2-Aligned)
docs.text: Component Purpose ATO Boundary Definition Template Defines UIAO as governance overlay, not system boundary Control Inheritance Matrix (NIST 800-53 Rev 5) Maps UIAO role per control (implements/inherits/supports) SSP Overlay Template UIAO-specific sections for insertion into agency SSPs Data Flow Templates Identity, addressing, routing, telemetry, governance overlay flows POA&M Drift Pattern Governance-level POA&M for drift detection AO Memo Insert Agency-agnostic memo for Authorizing Official ATO Accelerator Module (Appendix AT-04) Wraps all above into Phase 2 deliverable

docs.title: Thread 4: UIAO FedRAMP 22 Compliance / Document Compiler
Document Compiler v1.0 Architecture
docs.text:
YAML-driven, template-based system using MkDocs:

  • Canon: canon/uiao_leadership_briefing_v1.0.yaml (single source of truth)
  • Templates: Jinja2 .md.j2 files for each document type
  • Generation: Python script (scripts/generate_docs.py) renders YAML + templates -> Markdown
  • Build: MkDocs with Material theme
  • Deploy: GitHub Actions -> GitHub Pages

docs.title: Generated Documents from Canon
docs.text:
1. Leadership Briefing v1.0
  2. Program Vision v1.0
  3. Unified Architecture v1.0
  4. TIC 3.0 Roadmap v1.0
  5. Modernization Timeline v1.0
  6. FedRAMP 22 Compliance Summary v1.0

  9. Telemetry Plane Deep Dive v1.0

docs.title: Canon YAML Structure (Leadership Briefing)
docs.text: Top-level keys: version, document, classification, audience, leadership_briefing containing: - executive_summary, program_overview, modernization_need, program_vision - control_planes[] (5 planes: Identity, Network, Addressing, Telemetry/Location, Security/Compliance) - core_concepts[] (7 concepts: Conversation as Atomic Unit, Identity as Root Namespace, Deterministic Addressing, Certificate-Anchored Overlay, Telemetry as Control, Embedded Governance, Public Service First) - frozen_state, outcomes
